summary:
The Kingdom of Mysore (c. 1399–1947 AD, with its most significant period 1761–1799) was a South Indian state that rose to prominence under the military commanders Hyder Ali and his son [[event:3275|Tipu Sultan]], becoming the most serious indigenous military challenge to [[event:2358|British East India Company]] expansion in southern India. The Wadiyar dynasty of Mysore had ruled for centuries, but real power passed to Hyder Ali, a military commander of extraordinary ability who became de facto ruler in 1761 while maintaining the Wadiyar kings as nominal sovereigns. Hyder Ali modernized Mysore's army with French assistance, introduced rocket artillery on a large scale (Mysorean rockets were among the most effective of the era and influenced later European rocket development), and successfully fought the British in the First Anglo-Mysore War (1767–1769), forcing the Company to sue for peace.

Hyder Ali's son Tipu Sultan (r. 1782–1799) continued his father's modernization and resistance, corresponding with France and Napoleon, attempting to build an anti-British coalition, and introducing economic reforms including sericulture (silk production) and new crop cultivation. The four [[event:3274|Anglo-Mysore Wars]] (1767–1799) were among the most hard-fought conflicts of British Indian expansion; Mysore defeated the British in the first two and held its own in the third, before being overwhelmed in the Fourth (1799), when Tipu Sultan died defending his capital Seringapatam against a British-led force. Tipu's death ended Mysore's independence, and the restored Wadiyar dynasty became a subsidiary ally of the British. Tipu Sultan remains a complex national hero in India — celebrated by some as an anti-colonial resistance fighter, criticized by others for his forcible conversions of Hindus and Christians.
